title: Rescinding the subpoenas issued by the January 6th Select Committee on September 23, 2021, October 6, 2021, and February 9, 2022, and withdrawing the recommendations finding Stephen K. Bannon, Mark Randall Meadows, Daniel Scavino, Jr., and Peter K. Navarro in contempt of Congress.
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Rules, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently d
plain_summary: This resolution cancels subpoenas that the January 6th Select Committee sent to four men: Steve Bannon, Mark Meadows, Peter Navarro, and Dan Scavino. The committee wanted these men to testify about what happened on January 6th, 2021, when people attacked the U.S. Capitol building. When the men refused to cooperate, Congress said they were in contempt, which means they were breaking the law by not following orders. This resolution also removes those contempt findings and says the men no longer have to respond to the subpoenas.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Rules, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as f

sections.title: Resolution Text
sections.text: This resolution cancels certain subpoenas that were issued by the January sixth Select Committee and withdraws recommendations that found four people in contempt of Congress. The resolution states that the House of Representatives believes the Select Committee to Investigate the January sixth Attack on the United States Capitol was not legitimate. The committee's conclusions and findings were decided ahead of time because the committee was partisan (favored one political party). The resolution cancels the subpoenas that were issued on September twenty third two thousand twenty one, October sixth two thousand twenty one, and February ninth two thousand twenty two for Stephen K. Bannon, Mark Randall Meadows, Daniel Scavino Jr., and Peter K. Navarro. The resolution also withdraws three House resolutions that recommended finding these four men in contempt of Congress. House Resolution seven hundred thirty recommended finding Stephen K. Bannon in contempt and was adopted on October twenty first two thousand twenty one. House Resolution one thousand thirty seven recommended finding Peter K. Navarro and Daniel Scavino Jr. in contempt and was adopted on April sixth two thousand twenty two. House Resolution eight hundred fifty one recommended finding Mark Randall Meadows in contempt and was adopted on December fourteenth two thousand twenty one. All of these recommendations are withdrawn, dismissed, and ended without any further legal action or enforcement. Finally, the Speaker of the House of Representatives must notify the Department of Justice that the subpoenas are cancelled and should be considered null and void (having no legal effect).

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: The resolution provides background information about why it believes the January sixth Select Committee was flawed. On June thirtieth two thousand twenty one, Representative Nancy Pelosi, who was then Speaker of the House, created the Select Committee to Investigate the January sixth Attack on the United States Capitol. The resolution claims the committee was not properly formed because House Resolution five hundred three said the Speaker should appoint thirteen members to the committee, with five appointed after talking with the minority leader. However, Speaker Pelosi refused to accept the Republican members that Minority Leader Kevin McCarthy chose for the committee. This resulted in what the resolution calls a partisan exercise designed to blame President Trump and his advisors for the January sixth breach of the U.S. Capitol. Speaker Pelosi rejected McCarthy's choices and only named Representatives Liz Cheney and Adam Kinzinger to the committee, leaving it with nine members instead of the required thirteen. The resolution states that the committee held hearings, issued subpoenas (legal orders to provide testimony or documents), and published a flawed report without having the required number of members. Before hearings began, the committee named Representative Cheney as Vice Chair and operated without a ranking minority member (the top Republican member). The resolution argues that a vice chair is different from a ranking minority member according to House Rules and precedent. House Resolution five hundred three required the committee chair to consult with the ranking minority member in certain situations and could only issue subpoenas after consultation with the ranking minority member. Since there was no ranking minority member, the resolution claims all subpoenas issued by the committee were legally insufficient. The resolution also claims that Representative Bennie Thompson, who was the committee chair, failed to preserve all documents from the committee as required by House Rules, including documents, transcripts of witness interviews, and video recordings. The resolution states that the committee promoted theories without first checking if they were true by interviewing witnesses who had actual firsthand knowledge. It characterizes the committee as a partisan exercise from the beginning, with hearings and a final report that were tainted by Speaker Pelosi's unprecedented partisan decisions. The resolution claims that for nearly two years, the January sixth Committee presented unverified evidence that fit its narrative with the intent of disgracing President Trump, his advisors, and supporters to influence future elections. It also claims that committee members withheld and destroyed information that would have shown former President Donald Trump did not engage in an insurrection. Finally, the resolution states that the imprisonment of Peter Navarro and Steve Bannon represented an unprecedented attempt to silence and marginalize political opponents.
